stories.headline: Marcos calls Congress to special session on June 17 for nine priority bills and quake response
stories.summary: President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. issued Proclamation No. 1318 on June 15 convening Congress in a special session on June 17 to act on nine priority measures and ensure urgent assistance for communities hit by the June 8 Mindanao earthquake, GMA News reported. The bills include amendments to education-assistance laws, an anti-political dynasty measure, and health and scholarship programs. The session also gives the Senate a chance to address a leadership dispute that has gridlocked the chamber.
stories.context: The Senate has been split between a bloc led by Alan Peter Cayetano, who maintains he is still Senate president, and a new majority that recognizes Sherwin Gatchalian as acting Senate president. Malacanang has said it respects the new majority's choice.
stories.watch_for: Congress convenes the special session on June 17. Senator Joel Villanueva has said he will attend, which could give the Gatchalian bloc the votes to formally settle the leadership question.

stories.headline: House prosecutors file pre-trial brief in Sara Duterte impeachment as June 18 conference nears
stories.summary: House prosecutors filed their pre-trial brief on June 15 for the impeachment trial of Vice President Sara Duterte, listing more than 30 witnesses, lead prosecutor Batangas Rep. Gerville Luistro said, according to GMA News. The brief lays out the prosecution's plan for a case centered on an article alleging unexplained wealth and non-disclosure of assets in her statement of assets, liabilities and net worth. Some witness identities may be withheld for security reasons.
stories.context: The Senate, sitting as an impeachment court, directed both sides to submit pre-trial briefs by June 15. Acting Senate President Sherwin Gatchalian has set the pre-trial conference for June 18 at the Senate in Pasay City.
stories.watch_for: The pre-trial conference is set for June 18; the trial proper is scheduled to begin July 6.

stories.headline: Mindanao earthquake death toll rises to 65 with 624,000 people affected, OCD says
stories.summary: The Office of Civil Defense said the death toll from the magnitude 7.8 earthquake that struck off Maasim, Sarangani on June 8 rose to 65 as of June 15, with about 1,400 injured and 36 still missing. Roughly 624,000 people across 432 barangays have been affected, and infrastructure damage was estimated at P1.13 billion. The OCD said most deaths were caused by landslides and falling debris, and that search efforts were being redirected toward areas with better recovery prospects.
stories.context: About 14,200 people are sheltering in 37 evacuation centers and some 57,000 more outside them. The quake originated along the Cotabato Trench off southern Mindanao.

stories.headline: General Santos airport to resume commercial flights a week after Mindanao earthquake
stories.summary: The Department of Transportation and the Civil Aviation Authority of the Philippines said General Santos International Airport would resume commercial flights on June 15, a week after the June 8 magnitude 7.8 earthquake forced it to close. Transportation Secretary Giovanni Lopez said a full safety assessment cleared the facility to reopen. Only military, government and humanitarian flights were allowed during the closure, and passengers were advised to confirm schedules with their airlines.

stories.headline: BSP faces growth-versus-inflation dilemma ahead of June 18 rate decision
stories.summary: The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas is widely expected to raise its policy rate at its June 18 meeting, with most economists in a survey forecasting a 25-basis-point hike to 4.75 percent and a few, including HSBC and Standard Chartered, expecting a larger move, Manila Bulletin reported. Inflation eased to 6.8 percent in May from a more-than-three-year high of 7.2 percent in April, while first-quarter growth slowed to 2.8 percent, its weakest in five years, leaving the central bank weighing price pressures against a softening economy.
stories.context: Analysts cited the weak peso and the risk of a more hawkish US Federal Reserve as factors that could push the BSP toward a more aggressive stance.
stories.watch_for: The BSP Monetary Board announces its decision on the afternoon of June 18.

stories.headline: PAGASA reports easterlies and localized thunderstorms; no cyclone in the country's monitored area
stories.summary: The Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration said in its 4:00 p.m. bulletin on June 15 that easterlies were affecting the eastern sections of Visayas and Mindanao while localized thunderstorms affected the rest of the country. Eastern Visayas, the Dinagat Islands, Surigao del Norte and Sur, and Davao Oriental may see isolated rainshowers and thunderstorms, with risks of flash floods and landslides during severe activity. No tropical cyclone was present in the Philippine area of responsibility.

stories.headline: US and Iran reach framework deal to end war and reopen Strait of Hormuz, signing set for Friday
stories.summary: The United States and Iran reached a tentative agreement to end more than three months of war, including Israeli strikes on Lebanon, with the accord to be signed Friday in Switzerland, Al Jazeera reported. President Donald Trump said the deal was complete and that he would lift the US naval blockade and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, a key oil route Iran had effectively closed. The framework opens a 60-day window to negotiate sanctions relief and Iran's nuclear program, which remain unresolved.
stories.context: Qatar, Saudi Arabia and Turkey helped mediate, according to Pakistan's prime minister. The UN secretary-general welcomed the agreement, while some Israeli officials criticized it.
stories.watch_for: The agreement is due to be signed Friday in Switzerland, followed by negotiations over nuclear and sanctions issues during a 60-day window.

stories.headline: Asian stocks surge and oil falls after US-Iran peace framework
stories.summary: Asian stock markets rallied on June 15 after the United States and Iran announced a framework to end their war, with Japan's Nikkei average climbing toward 70,000 and South Korea's Kospi closing about 4.6 percent higher, Nikkei Asia reported. Oil prices dropped, with Brent crude falling roughly 4 percent, after President Trump said the Strait of Hormuz would reopen. Analysts cautioned that energy prices could stay elevated for months as risk premiums unwind.
stories.context: Iran had effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz for months, choking off a route used for about a fifth of the world's oil and pushing up global energy prices.

stories.headline: G7 summit opens in Evian with Iran deal, Ukraine and trade high on the agenda
stories.summary: Leaders of the Group of Seven gathered in Evian-les-Bains, France, for a summit running June 15 to 17, with the newly announced US-Iran framework, Russia's war in Ukraine, trade tensions and competition with China on the agenda, Al Jazeera reported. The bloc, comprising Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, the UK and the US, plus the EU, was joined by guests including Ukraine's president and several Middle Eastern and Asian leaders. The gathering drew large protests in nearby Geneva.
stories.context: France holds the rotating G7 presidency. The summit follows the US-Iran agreement announced on the eve of the meeting.
stories.watch_for: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy is due to join a working session on Ukraine during the summit, which runs through June 17.
